title: BifurcationAlternative

tex_statement.html: Let $G = \lt J, V, E, L \rt$ be a CDMG. For $v, w, c \in V \cup J$: there exists a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ with source $c$ if and only if $v \ne w$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$.

% Claim proof file for row claim_3_5 -- "BifurcationAlternative".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_5 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: BifurcationAlternative
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_5}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_5}
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Prp}[BifurcationAlternative]\label{prp:bifurcations_alternative}
  Let $G = \lt J, V, E, L \rt$ be a CDMG. For $v, w, c \in V \cup J$:
  there exists a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ with source $c$ if and only if $v \ne w$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$.
\end{Prp}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% TODO: write the proof body.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Proposition â€” BifurcationAlternative

Let $G = \lt J, V, E, L \rt$ be a CDMG. For $v, w, c \in V \cup J$: there exists a bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ with source $c$ if and only if $v \ne w$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v) \sm \{v\}$ and $c \in \Anc^{G_{\doit(v)}}(w) \sm \{w\}$.

Proof.

lean.name: bifurcation_alternative
lean.code:
/-- LN claim 3.5 (`prp:bifurcations_alternative`): for vertices
`v`, `w`, `c` of a CDMG `G`, there exists a bifurcation between
`v` and `w` in `G` with source `c` iff `v â‰  w` and both
ancestor-set conditions
`c âˆˆ Anc^{G_{do(w)}}(v) âˆ– {v}` and `c âˆˆ Anc^{G_{do(v)}}(w) âˆ– {w}`
hold. The LHS quantifies existentially over a walk
`Ï€ : Walk G v w` and a witness `hb : Ï€.IsBifurcation`, with the
source extracted via `Ï€.bifurcationSource hb = some c`; an extra
`c â‰  w` conjunct on the LHS captures the LN's *implicit*
"non-trivial directed path from `c` to `w`" requirement at the
claim level (see `## Design choice` below).

## Design choice

* **The `c â‰  w` LHS conjunct is the load-bearing decision for
  this row.** Without it, the iff is *literally false*. The
  current `Walk.bifurcationSource`
  (`Section3_1/Bifurcation.lean` lines 362 -- 367) returns
  `some bw.m'` whenever the chosen witness's hinge is
  `.backward _`, regardless of whether the witness's `rightArm`
  is the trivial `.nil` walk. Consequently, an all-backward walk
  `v âŠ£ â‹¯ âŠ£ w` (length â‰¥ 1) is an `IsBifurcation` whose source is
  `w` (the witness picks the last backward step as the hinge,
  `rightArm = .nil w`, so `bw.m' = w`): the LHS with `c = w`
  holds, but the RHS forbids `c = w` via the `\ {w}` exclusion.
  Two repair routes were considered.
  - **Path 1** -- tighten `bifurcationSource` itself to require
    a non-trivial `rightArm` in addition to the backward hinge.
    **Rejected.** That extractor is owned by **def_3_4**
    (`Section3_1/Bifurcation.lean`, a previously-solved row in
    a different subsection); editing it from a Section_3_2 row
    breaks the manager-side scope rule, and the heavy-redesign
    machinery would have to be invoked to retract def_3_4 and
    its dependents. Disproportionate surgery to make one iff
    hold.
  - **Path 2** (chosen) -- add `c â‰  w` to the LHS so it mirrors
    the RHS's `\ {w}` exclusion clause-for-clause. Local,
    surgical, LN-faithful: the LN's own proof prose at
    `graphs.tex` lines 366 -- 371 calls the right half a
    *"non-trivial directed path from `c` to `w`"*, and `c â‰  w`
    is exactly the Lean transcription of "non-trivial". The LN's
    def_3_4 item 6 leaves this implicit -- claim_3_5 is where
    the LN first relies on it -- so encoding it on the claim's
    LHS rather than retroactively tightening def_3_4 stays
    closer to the LN's own factoring.

  See the *Manager decision (2026-05-20)* section at the top of
  `Section3_2/workspace_claim_3_5.md` for the full decision
  record (counterexample, scope-rule discussion, forward-tax
  estimate).

* **No symmetric `c â‰  v` conjunct on the LHS -- deliberate
  asymmetry.** `IsBifurcation` already enforces
  `v âˆ‰ Ï€.support.tail` (`Section3_1/Bifurcation.lean` line 311).
  For any witness `bw` of `Ï€ : Walk G v w`, the source vertex
  `c = bw.m'` sits at position `1 + bw.leftArm.length â‰¥ 1` of
  `Ï€.support`, i.e. inside `Ï€.support.tail`; combined with
  `v âˆ‰ Ï€.support.tail` this forces `c â‰  v` automatically. The
  asymmetry between the two endpoints mirrors how
  `bifurcationSource` *extracts* the source -- it names `m'`,
  the right side of the hinge -- so it is the *right arm* whose
  non-triviality has to be enforced separately on the LHS. The
  left arm's non-triviality is irrelevant to the source
  extractor: even a degenerate `k = 1` (empty `leftArm`) witness
  does not threaten the iff.

* **Two-argument `Anc` and singleton hard interventions.** `Anc`
  is the two-argument form `Anc : CDMG Î± â†’ Î± â†’ Set Î±`
  (`Section3_1/FamilyReachability.lean` lines 115 -- 125), not a
  one-argument predicate bundled over an implicit subgraph. The
  LN's `\Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v)` therefore transcribes as
  `Anc (G.hardInterventionOn ({w} : Set Î±)) v`: the intervened
  graph is supplied as the first positional argument, and the
  singleton `{w}` is a literal `Set Î±`. We reuse the existing
  `hardInterventionOn : CDMG Î± â†’ Set Î± â†’ CDMG Î±` API of
  `Section3_2/HardInterventionOn.lean` directly rather than
  introducing a vertex-flavoured `hardInterventionOnVertex`
  wrapper -- the LN's notation is set-flavoured (the more
  general `G_{\doit(W)}` is the actual definitional unit), and
  the single-vertex case is just a one-element instantiation.
  Note also that `G.hardInterventionOn ({w} : Set Î±)` is
  well-defined for any `w : Î±` (no `w âˆˆ G` precondition),
  matching `HardInterventionOn`'s no-precondition design -- a
  design choice that was explicitly justified, in part, by this
  claim's need to write `G.hardInterventionOn {w}` without
  having to discharge `w âˆˆ G` at every call site.

* **No explicit `v, w, c âˆˆ G` precondition.** The LN's preamble
  "For `v, w, c âˆˆ V âˆª J`" is *not* hoisted into a hypothesis on
  the Lean statement. Both sides of the iff already force the
  relevant memberships where they are load-bearing:
  - LHS: a `Ï€ : Walk G v w` with `IsBifurcation` has length â‰¥ 1
    (`length_pos_of_isBifurcation`,
    `Section3_1/Bifurcation.lean` lines 395 -- 399), and its
    first/last/hinge edges place `v`, `w`, `c` in `G` via
    `G.E_subset` / `CDMG.mem_iff`.
  - RHS: each `c âˆˆ Anc G' v` membership carries a built-in
    `c âˆˆ G'` clause (`mem_Anc`,
    `Section3_1/FamilyReachability.lean` lines 122 -- 125),
    and the `\ {v}` / `\ {w}` exclusions give the remaining
    `c â‰  v` / `c â‰  w` constraints.
  An explicit precondition would therefore be redundant on both
  sides and would mildly clutter the consumer surface at every
  call site that does not already have the memberships in hand.
  Same no-precondition pattern as `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn`
  (`Section3_2/AcyclicUnderIntervention.lean` line 191) and the
  `hardInterventionOn_hardInterventionOn` / `_comm` pair in
  `Section3_2/HardInterventionsCommute.lean`.

* **Walk direction: `Walk G v w`, not `Walk G w v`.** Matches
  the LN's def 3.4 ordering `v = v_0 \hus \cdots \hus v_n = w`
  -- the start vertex of the walk is the LN's `v`, the end
  vertex is the LN's `w`. This lets the `BifurcationWitness`'s
  `leftArm` / `rightArm` decomposition line up with the LN's
  left-of-hinge / right-of-hinge arms without any direction
  inversion, and lets `bw.m'` (the source) read off as the
  LN's `v_k` on the right of the hinge. The arrow direction on
  individual edges is independent of the walk direction --
  steps can be `.forward`, `.backward`, or `.bidir` either way
  -- so this choice is purely about index orientation, not
  about which arrows the walk is allowed to contain.

* **Known limitation / future redesign trigger.** The `c â‰  w`
  conjunct on the LHS is a *local* compensation for the
  looseness of `bifurcationSource` in the
  all-backward-walk corner case. Any future downstream claim
  that re-phrases "bifurcation with source `c`" in an
  *asymmetric* shape -- relating the source to ancestral /
  acyclicity / topological-order properties of one specific
  endpoint -- will need to repeat the same `c â‰  w` conjunct (or
  its symmetric `c â‰  v` variant) on its own LHS. Symmetric
  claims (e.g. the marginalisation-preserves-bifurcations
  reading of claim_3_6, which only quantifies source *existence*
  rather than identifying the source with a specific endpoint)
  are corner-case-stable and need no extra bookkeeping. If
  enough downstream rows end up paying this asymmetry tax, the
  **Path 1** refactor (tighten `bifurcationSource` once at the
  source, propagate via the heavy-redesign machinery) should be
  re-considered. -/
theorem bifurcation_alternative {G : CDMG Î±} {v w c : Î±} :
    (âˆƒ Ï€ : Walk G v w, âˆƒ hb : Ï€.IsBifurcation,
       Ï€.bifurcationSource hb = some c âˆ§ c â‰  w)
    â†” v â‰  w
      âˆ§ c âˆˆ Anc (G.hardInterventionOn ({w} : Set Î±)) v \ {v}
      âˆ§ c âˆˆ Anc (G.hardInterventionOn ({v} : Set Î±)) w \ {w} := by

lean_explanation:
The theorem fixes a CDMG `G : CDMG Î±` over an ambient vertex type `Î± : Type*` â€” the LN's silent "$G = (J, V, E, L)$ a CDMG" carries no built-in finiteness and none is needed here. The three vertices `v w c : Î±` are the LN's $v$, $w$, $c$ from the preamble "For $v, w, c \in V \cup J$". The walk `Ï€ : Walk G v w` is indexed in the LN's left-to-right order: its start vertex is $v$, its end vertex is $w$, so the witness's `leftArm` / `rightArm` decomposition lines up with the LN's left-of-hinge / right-of-hinge arms without any direction inversion. Individual steps can independently be `.forward`, `.backward`, or `.bidir` â€” the walk direction here is index orientation, not arrow orientation.

On the LHS, `Ï€.IsBifurcation` (def_3_4) bundles the LN's full bifurcation data: endpoints distinct, neither endpoint reappearing in the interior of the walk's support, and a (squashed) `BifurcationWitness` decomposing $\pi$ as $L \hus h \hus R$ with $L$ all-backward, $h$ pointing into the source, and $R$ directed. The Lean extractor `Ï€.bifurcationSource hb` returns `some bw.m'` when the chosen witness's hinge is `.backward _` (and `none` otherwise), so the equation `Ï€.bifurcationSource hb = some c` pins $c$ down as the LN's source vertex $v_k$. The trailing `c â‰  w` is the load-bearing extra conjunct discussed in the design notes â€” it transcribes the LN's *implicit* "non-trivial directed path from $c$ to $w$" requirement at this claim's call site.

On the RHS, `Anc` is the two-argument form `Anc : CDMG Î± â†’ Î± â†’ Set Î±` (def_3_3), so the LN's $\Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v)$ becomes `Anc (G.hardInterventionOn ({w} : Set Î±)) v`: the intervened graph is the first positional argument and the singleton $\{w\}$ is a literal `Set Î±` (`G.hardInterventionOn` is the def_3_10 hard-intervention construction, which takes a set of intervention targets â€” the single-vertex case is just a one-element instantiation). The set-minus `\ {v}` is `Set.diff` with a singleton, exactly the LN's $\setminus \{v\}$.

The shape is the standard "existence of a witness on the left, two ancestor memberships on the right" iff. Downstream callers reach for `.mp` (bifurcation data â‡’ ancestor conditions) or `.mpr` (ancestor conditions â‡’ bifurcation data) â€” the latter constructs an explicit witness by joining the reversed ancestor walk to $v$ with a backward hinge edge to the ancestor walk to $w$.
design_choices:
**The `c â‰  w` LHS conjunct is the load-bearing decision for this row.** Without it the iff is *literally false*. The extractor `Walk.bifurcationSource` returns `some bw.m'` whenever the chosen witness's hinge is `.backward _`, regardless of whether the witness's `rightArm` is the trivial `.nil` walk. Consequently an all-backward walk $v \hus \cdots \hus w$ (length $\geq 1$) is itself a bifurcation whose extracted source is $w$ (the witness can pick the last backward step as the hinge and `rightArm = .nil w`, making `bw.m' = w`): the LHS with $c = w$ holds, but the RHS forbids $c = w$ via the $\setminus \{w\}$ exclusion. Two repair routes were considered. The first â€” tighten `bifurcationSource` itself to require a non-trivial `rightArm` in addition to the backward hinge â€” was rejected: that extractor lives in def_3_4 in Section 3.1, and editing it from a Section 3.2 row would have meant retracting def_3_4 and propagating through every downstream consumer, disproportionate surgery to make one iff hold. The chosen route adds `c â‰  w` to the LHS so it mirrors the RHS's $\setminus \{w\}$ clause-for-clause; the LN's own proof prose calls the right half a "non-trivial directed path from $c$ to $w$", and `c â‰  w` is exactly the Lean transcription of "non-trivial". The LN leaves this implicit at def_3_4 â€” claim 3.5 is the first place it relies on it â€” so encoding it on the claim's LHS rather than retroactively tightening def_3_4 stays closer to the LN's own factoring.

**No symmetric `c â‰  v` conjunct on the LHS â€” deliberate asymmetry.** `IsBifurcation` already enforces $v \notin \pi$`.support.tail`. For any witness $bw$ of $\pi : $`Walk G v w`, the source vertex `c = bw.m'` sits at position $1 + $`bw.leftArm.length` $\geq 1$ of $\pi$`.support`, i.e. inside the tail; combined with $v \notin \pi$`.support.tail` this forces `c â‰  v` automatically. The asymmetry mirrors how `bifurcationSource` extracts the source â€” it names `m'`, the right side of the hinge â€” so it is the *right arm* whose non-triviality has to be enforced separately on the LHS. The left arm's non-triviality is irrelevant to the extractor: even a degenerate empty-`leftArm` witness does not threaten the iff.

**Two-argument `Anc` and set-flavoured hard interventions.** `Anc` is the two-argument form `Anc : CDMG Î± â†’ Î± â†’ Set Î±` rather than a one-argument predicate bundled over an implicit subgraph; the LN's $\Anc^{G_{\doit(w)}}(v)$ transcribes as `Anc (G.hardInterventionOn ({w} : Set Î±)) v`. The existing set-flavoured `hardInterventionOn : CDMG Î± â†’ Set Î± â†’ CDMG Î±` is used directly rather than introducing a vertex-flavoured `hardInterventionOnVertex` wrapper â€” the LN's notation is set-flavoured (the general $G_{\doit(W)}$ is the actual definitional unit), and the single-vertex case is just a one-element instantiation. `G.hardInterventionOn ({w} : Set Î±)` is also well-defined for any `w : Î±` without a `w âˆˆ G` precondition â€” a design choice from `HardInterventionOn` that was explicitly justified, in part, by this claim's need to write `G.hardInterventionOn {w}` without discharging $w \in G$ at every call site.

**No explicit `v, w, c âˆˆ G` precondition.** The LN's preamble "For $v, w, c \in V \cup J$" is not hoisted into a Lean hypothesis. Both sides of the iff already force the relevant memberships where they are load-bearing: on the LHS a bifurcation walk has length $\geq 1$ and its first/last/hinge edges place $v$, $w$, $c$ in $G$ via the edge-source inclusion; on the RHS each `c âˆˆ Anc G' v` membership carries a built-in `c âˆˆ G'` clause, and the $\setminus \{v\}$ / $\setminus \{w\}$ exclusions give the remaining constraints. An explicit precondition would be redundant on both sides and would mildly clutter the consumer surface at every call site that does not already have the memberships in hand. Same no-precondition pattern as `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn` and the `hardInterventionOn_hardInterventionOn` / `_comm` pair earlier in the section.

**Walk direction `Walk G v w`, not `Walk G w v`.** Matches the LN's def 3.4 ordering $v = v_0 \hus \cdots \hus v_n = w$ â€” the start vertex is $v$, the end vertex is $w$. This lets the witness's `leftArm` / `rightArm` line up with the LN's left-of-hinge / right-of-hinge arms without direction inversion, and lets `bw.m'` (the source) read off as the LN's $v_k$ on the right of the hinge. The arrow direction on individual edges is independent of the walk direction â€” steps can be `.forward`, `.backward`, or `.bidir` either way â€” so this is purely about index orientation, not about which arrows are allowed.

**Known limitation / future redesign trigger.** The `c â‰  w` LHS conjunct is a *local* compensation for the looseness of `bifurcationSource` in the all-backward-walk corner case. Any future downstream claim that re-phrases "bifurcation with source $c$" in an *asymmetric* shape â€” relating the source to ancestral or topological-order properties of one specific endpoint â€” will need to repeat the same conjunct on its own LHS. Symmetric claims that only quantify source *existence* (rather than identifying the source with a specific endpoint) are corner-case-stable and need no extra bookkeeping. If enough downstream rows end up paying this asymmetry tax, the heavier refactor (tighten `bifurcationSource` once at the source and propagate through consumers) should be reconsidered.
